courses.description: ANTHR 102-CULTURAL ANTHROPOLOGY 3 UNITS 54.00 Lecture Hours, 108 Out-of-Class Hours, Total Student Learning Hours=162 Cultural anthropology examines the broad conditions and experiences of being human through the lens of culture and the difference it makes. This course introduces the methods, theories and insights of cultural anthropology and their application to life in a multicultural society. Topics include, but are not limited to: the research and analysis of culture and cultural processes; cross-cultural comparisons of subsistence patterns, economics, kinship, gender, language, sexuality, political organization, belief systems, and expressive culture; the production of social identities and inequalities; and, cultural change in an interconnected world affected by colonization and globalization. Introduction to California's unique geography; examining political, economic, cultural, physical, and historical processes and characteristics. Field trips might be required. (A-F or P/NP) Transfer: (CSU, UC) General Education: Using catalog rights, check catalog for course general education approvals. For further clarification, see a counselor. (C-ID: GEOG 140)

courses.title: World Regional Geography
courses.instructor: Marcelo Santos Rocha Da Silva, Michelle R. Walsh
courses.description: GEOG 110-WORLD REGIONAL GEOGRAPHY, 3 UNITS 54 Lecture Hours, 108 Out-of-Class Hours, Total Student Learning Hours=162 Recommended for Success: Before enrolling in this course, students are strongly advised to satisfactorily complete ENGL C1000 (formerly ENGL 101) or ENGL C1000E (formerly ENGL 100) or ELIC 100. Survey of the world's major geographical regions and their physical, economic, political, and cultural characteristics. Emphasis is placed upon historical influences which explain current problems and conditions. Field trips are not required. (A-F or P/NP) Not repeatable. Transfer: (CSU, UC) General Education: Using catalog rights, check catalog for course general education approvals. For further clarification, see a counselor. (C-ID: GEOG 125)

courses.title: Epidemiology
courses.description: HE 108 - EPIDEMIOLOGY 3 UNITS 54 LECTURE HOURS, 108 OUT-OF-CLASS HOURS, 162 TOTAL STUDENT LEARNING HOURS Before enrolling in this course, students are strongly advised to have satisfactorily complete ENGL C1000E or ENGL C1000 or ELIC 100. This introductory course examines the social, cultural, political, and historical impact of epidemics and disease outbreaks. It will include the applications of epidemiology to public health practice and our society, and the study of the ethical and social context of epidemiology through great works of literature and selected historical narratives. Field trips are not required. Transfer: (CSU, UC) Not repeatable. General Education: Using catalog rights, check catalog for course general education approvals. For further clarification, see a counselor. (C-ID: PH 108)

courses.title: Global Health
courses.description: HE 109 - GLOBAL HEALTH 3 UNITS 54 LECTURE HOURS, 108 OUT-OF-CLASS HOURS, 162 TOTAL STUDENT LEARNING HOURS Introduces the growing field of global health, global health challenges, programs, and policies through theoretical foundations and research methods. Students will explore how health, illness, and well-being are shaped by social, cultural, political, economic, and environmental forces worldwide. Topics include comparative health systems; emerging global health priorities; social determinants of health; health systems reform; and major global health initiatives for disease prevention and health promotion. An emphasis is placed on global health ethics, health inequities, and issues of social justice. Field trips are not required. Not repeatable. Transfer: (CSU, UC) Grading: A-F or P/NP. General Education: Using catalog rights, check catalog for course general education approvals. For further clarification, see a counselor. (C-ID PH 109)

courses.title: Tax Accounting
courses.description: BUSAD 336-TAX ACCOUNTING 4 UNITS 72 Lecture Hours, 144 Out-of-Class Hours, Total Student Learning Hours=216 Open to both accounting and non-accounting majors. This class covers the federal and California personal income tax laws. Study includes a practical user approach to the most important areas of the tax law including how to calculate individual income tax, maximize deductions and credits, and tax planning strategies. Emphasis placed on compliance, basic preparation and learning how to research tax questions and interpret tax laws and regulations as they relate to the individual taxpayer. Field trips are not required. Not repeatable. (A-F or P/NP)

courses.title: Introduction to Accounting With Quickbooks
courses.instructor: Jennifer A. Stueve, Patricia D. Wall
courses.description: BUSAD 310-INTRODUCTION TO ACCOUNTING WITH QUICKBOOKS, 3 UNITS Formerly listed as: BUSAD 310: Bookkeeping 1 54 Lecture Hours, 108 Out-of-Class Hours, Total Student Learning Hours=162 Covers accounting concepts and procedures required to complete the full accounting cycle for a service company and a merchandising firm using generally accepted accounting principles (GAAP) in a manual and computerized environment. The emphasis is on recording transactions, preparing financial statements, performing bank reconciliations, and processing payroll for a sole proprietorship. After learning to complete those tasks manually, students create a company file, maintain the chart of accounts, complete a full accounting cycle, and reconcile the bank account using QuickBooks. Field trips are not required. Not repeatable. (A-F or P/NP) (C-ID: BSOT 127 X)

courses.title: Exploration of Health Professions
courses.description: HE 105 - EXPLORATION OF HEALTH PROFESSIONS 3 UNITS 54.00 Lecture Hours, 108.00 Out-of-Class Hours, 162.00 Total Student Learning Hours This course provides an overview of health care careers and their respective career paths, educational and skill requirements, and professional responsibilities. Basic skills required by health-related careers, such as emphasizing personal attributes, demonstrating professionalism, engaging in teamwork, and building communication skills will be covered. Students will be assisted in making educational and career decisions for a wide variety of health care occupations. Not repeatable. (A-F or P/NP) Transfers to CSU. Field trips are not required. courses.title: Spanish for Spanish Speakers 1
courses.description: SPAN 109-SPANISH FOR SPANISH SPEAKERS 1 5 UNITS 90 Lecture Hours, 180 Out-of-Class Hours, Total Student Learning Hours=270 Prerequisite: Satisfactory completion of SPAN 102. Recommended for Success: Before enrolling in this course, students are strongly advised to understand and be able to communicate in Spanish with near native fluency. The first of two courses intended for Native or Heritage Spanish speakers who already speak, read and write in Spanish at varying levels and with some academic study in the language. This course will focus on: the review of major elements of Spanish grammar, vocabulary acquisition, and student improvement of oral and written communication skills. Through the study of selected readings, students will expand on their own experiences and explore other Spanish Speaking cultures. Equivalent to the satisfactory completion of two years high school Spanish. Taught in Spanish. Field trips might be required. Not repeatable. (A-F or P/NP) Transfer: (CSU, UC) (C-ID: SPAN 220) General Education: Using catalog rights, check catalog for course general education approvals. For further clarification, see a counselor.

courses.title: Philosophy
courses.instructor: Tristin Hassell, Bill Anelli, George W. Shea
courses.description: PHILO 101-PHILOSOPHY, 3 UNITS 54 Lecture Hours, 108 Out-of-Class Hours, Total Student Learning Hours=162 Recommended for Success: Before enrolling in this course, students are strongly advised to satisfactorily complete ENGL C1000E (formerly ENGL 100) or ENGL C1000 (formerly ENGL 101) or ELIC 100. A careful and critical examination of some of the Great Questions philosophers have pursued from ancient times to the present concerning the nature of reality, knowledge, and value. Some of these include: What is real? What is human nature? Do we have free will? Does God exist? What can we know? How should we act? What is the ideal community? And, what is the nature of truth? Course also includes how to think philosophically. Field trips might be required. (A-F or P/NP)Not repeatable. Transfer:(CSU, UC) (CC: PHILO 1) General Education: Using catalog rights, check catalog for course general education approvals. For further clarification, see a counselor. (C-ID: PHIL 100)

courses.title: Spanish for Spanish Speakers 2
courses.description: SPAN 110-SPANISH FOR SPANISH SPEAKERS 2 5 UNITS 90 Lecture Hours, 180 Out-of-Class Hours, Total Student Learning Hours=270 Prerequisite: Satisfactory completion of SPAN 109. A continuation of SPAN 109. This course is intended for Spanish-speaking students who seek to continue building their reading, writing, speaking, and listening skills in standard Spanish. Students will continue to increase awareness of linguistic registers, and discuss topics beyond the familiar routine through continued grammar review, vocabulary expansion and writing. Students will continue to expand upon their appreciation for Spanish speaking cultures through the discussion and analysis of selected readings, in Spanish, from Spanish speaking countries and the United States. Equivalent to the satisfactory completion of three years of high school Spanish. Taught exclusively in Spanish. Field trips might be required. (A-F or P/NP) Transfer: (CSU, UC) General Education: Using catalog rights, check catalog for course general education approvals. For further clarification, see a counselor. (C-ID: SPAN 230)

courses.title: Drugs, Health, and Society
courses.instructor: Zeb Brayton, Rusty N. Stivers, Milan C. Motroni, Shawn A. Black, Paul J. Brogan, Kelly Nasrawi
courses.description: HE 115-DRUGS, HEALTH, AND SOCIETY 3 UNITS 54 Lecture Hours, 108 Out-of-Class Hours, Total Student Learning Hours=162 Explores substance use and abuse, focusing on how individual behavior, social environments, and public health interact. Topics include the epidemiology and toxicology of substance use, psychological and social factors influencing behavior, and the effects of licit and illicit drugs on the brain. Students will analyze patterns and trends in substance use, examine risk and protective factors, and evaluate prevention, recovery, and community-based interventions. Field trips are not required. Not repeatable. (A-F or P/NP) Transfer: (CSU, UC) General Education: Using catalog rights, check catalog for course general education approvals. For further clarification, see a counselor. (C-ID: PH 103)

courses.title: Introduction to Public Health
courses.instructor: Rusty N. Stivers, Heidi A. Santino, Zeb Brayton, Milan C. Motroni, Mary Shea, Kimberly Montague, Michael Girardi
courses.description: HE 112-INTRODUCTION TO PUBLIC HEALTH 3 UNITS 54 Lecture Hours, 108 Out-of-Class Hours, Total Student Learning Hours=162 Introduces public health, emphasizing how social, cultural, economic, and environmental factors influence health behaviors and outcomes. Topics include population health challenges, social determinants of health, epidemiology of infectious and chronic diseases, prevention and control strategies, community health promotion, environmental health, global health, and healthcare policy. Students will explore strategies to reduce health disparities and promote health equity across populations. Field trips are not required. Not repeatable. (A-F or P/NP) Transfer: (CSU, UC) General Education: Using catalog rights, check catalog for course general education approvals. For further clarification, see a counselor.(C-ID: PH 101)

courses.title: Social Determinants of Health, Disparities and Equities
courses.description: HE 113 - SOCIAL DETERMINATION OF HEALTH, DISPARITIES AND EQUITIES 3 UNITS 54 LECTURE HOURS, 108 OUT-OF-CLASS HOURS, 162 TOTAL STUDENT LEARNING HOURS Recommendation for Success: Before enrolling in this course, students are strongly advised to have satisfactorily complete ENGL C1000E or ENGL C1000 or ELIC 100. Examination of personal and social determinants of health through the examination of the role individuals play in their own health and wellbeing, along with the impact of environmental, social, structural, and cultural factors that play a role both in individual and community and public health. Health disparities and inequities in sexually diverse, gender, racial, and minority populations will be explored, and culturally competent ideas to bridge the health equity gap and improve population health will be developed. Not repeatable. Transfer: (CSU, UC). Field trips are not required. (A-F or P/NP) General Education: Using catalog rights, check catalog for course general education approvals. For further clarification, see a counselor.(C-ID: PH 113)

courses.title: Healthful Living
courses.instructor: David L. Shrock, Steve A. Aristotelous, Michael Girardi, Paul J. Brogan, Jim W. Stevens, Kelly Nasrawi, Heidi A. Santino, Shawn A. Black, Amanda K. Matis, Zach Hollis, Jesse Vazquez, Eric D. Fischer
courses.description: HE 110-HEALTHFUL LIVING 3 UNITS, 54 Lecture Hours, 108 Out-of-Class Hours, Total Student Learning Hours=162 This course will require students to explore, analyze, personalize, and discuss the following issues as they relate to the essential components of health and wellness: nutrition, physical activity/exercise/fitness, weight control, eating disorders and body image, media influences, mental health, stress, violence, substance use/abuse, sexuality and sexual orientation, sexually transmitted infections, reproductive choices/contraception, relationships, disease prevention, environment, health care, aging, and general public health issues. Students will be taught the knowledge and skills necessary to implement lifestyle behaviors that can improve their health and well-being. Field trips are not required. Not repeatable. (A-F or P/NP) Transfer: (CSU, UC) (CC: HHP 60) General Education: Using catalog rights, check catalog for course general education approvals. For further clarification, see a counselor. (C-ID: PH 100)

courses.title: Social Problems in the United States
courses.instructor: Ramin Mirfakhraie, Holly Suarez, Jill K. Biondo, Tracey L. Potts, SR
courses.description: SOCIO 102-SOCIAL PROBLEMS IN THE UNITED STATES 3 UNITS, 54 Lecture Hours, 108 Out-of-Class Hours, Total Student Learning Hours=162 Recommended for Success: Before enrolling in this course, students are strongly advised to satisfactorily complete SOCIO 101. The study of contemporary social problems within the American society emphasizing, among the other topics, alcohol and drugs crime and violence, education, medicine, health family problems, power, class, ethnicity, and gender inequalities. Construction of possible solutions to social problems will also be analyzed within a classic and contemporary theoretical sociological framework. Field trips might be required. Not repeatable. (A-F or P/NP) Transfer: (CSU, UC) (CC: SOCIO 2) General Education: Using catalog rights, check catalog for course general education approvals. For further clarification, see a counselor. (C-ID: SOCI 115)

courses.title: Psychosocial Rehabilitation Practice
courses.description: SWHS 143-PSYCHOSOCIAL REHABILITATION PRACTICE 3 UNITS 54 Lecture Hours, 108 Out-of-Class Hours, Total Student Learning Hours=162 (Formerly listed as: HUMSR 143) Prerequisite: Satisfactory completion of SWHS 142 (Formerly HUMSR 142). This course is designed to expand students' prior learning in the field of psychosocial rehabilitation, and its application in the public mental health system. Students will have an opportunity to practice and apply models of psychosocial rehabilitation, principles, theories, and methods related to the social sciences with individuals who have psychiatric disorders using sociological concepts and methodology. Formerly listed as HUMSR 143.Field trips might be required. Not repeatable. (A-F or P/NP) Transfer: (CSU) General Education: Using catalog rights, check catalog for course general education approvals. For further clarification, see a counselor.

meta.prerequisites: Prerequisite: Satisfactory completion of SWHS 142 (Formerly HUMSR 142).

courses.title: Co-Occurring Disorders
courses.description: SWHS 113-CO-OCCURRING DISORDERS 3 UNITS (Formerly listed as: HUMSR 113) 54 Lecture Hours, 108 Out-of-Class Hours, Total Student Learning Hours=162 Recommended for Success: Before enrolling in this course, students are strongly advised to satisfactorily complete ENGL C1000E (formerly ENGL 100) or ENGL C1000 (formerly ENGL 101) or ELIC 100. This course reviews the major concepts and features associated with addiction. Skills in recognizing co-occurring disorders, referral and case management of clients, and appropriate scope of practice are emphasized. Common types of mental health issues associated with addiction and listed in the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (DSM-V), including mood, anxiety, adjustment disorders, trauma disorders, and unresolved issues of adverse childhood experiences are covered as well as an overview of appropriate treatment and management approaches. Formerly listed as HUMSR 113. Field trips might be required. Not repeatable. (A-F Only) Transfer: (CSU)

courses.title: Introduction to Psychosocial Rehabilitation
courses.description: SWHS 142-INTRODUCTION TO PSYCHOSOCIAL REHABILITATION 3 UNITS 54 Lecture Hours, 108 Out-of-Class Hours, Total Student Learning Hours=162 (Formerly listed as: HUMSR 142) Recommended for Success: Before enrolling in this course, students are strongly advised to satisfactorily complete SWHS 110 (formerly listed as: HUMSR 110) and satisfactorily complete ENGL C1000E (formerly ENGL 100) or ENGL C1000 (formerly ENGL 101) or ELIC 100. Introduction to the field of psychosocial rehabilitation and its application in the public mental health system. The course provides an overview of the core practice models, principles, theories, and methods in psychosocial rehabilitation as related to the social sciences, and gives students a broad view of best clinical practices, social and psychological considerations in working with individuals who have psychiatric disorders using sociological concepts, theories, and methodology. Formerly listed as HUMSR 142. Field trips might be required. Not repeatable. (A-F or P/NP) Transfer: (CSU) General Education: See course in catalog for general education approvals.

courses.title: Introduction to Corrections
courses.description: ADJU 235-INTRODUCTION TO CORRECTIONS 3 UNITS 54 Lecture Hours, 108 Out-of-Class Hours, Total Student Learning Hours=162 Introduction to corrections is designed to give the student an understanding of the concepts of criminal parole, probation and the correctional system. courses.title: Introduction to Western Religions
courses.description: HUMAN 130-INTRODUCTION TO WESTERN RELIGIONS, 3 UNITS 54 Lecture Hours, 108 Out-of-Class Hours, Total Student Learning Hours=162 Recommended for Success: Before enrolling in this course, students are strongly advised to satisfactorily complete ENGL C1000E (formerly ENGL 100) or satisfactorily complete ENGL C1000 (formerly ENGL 101). Origins and development of the three monotheistic religions of Western Civilization, Judaism, Christianity and Islam; scripture, beliefs, traditions, rituals, and celebrations; scripture of all three faiths, along with architecture and arts. courses.title: Symbolic Logic
courses.instructor: George W. Shea, Jennifer D. Smith
courses.description: PHILO 103-SYMBOLIC LOGIC, 3 UNITS Also offered as: CSCI 203 54 Lecture Hours, 108 Out-of-Class Hours, Total Student Learning Hours=162 Is there a mathematical basis to thinking? Can grammar and an individual's everyday way of speaking be expressed more clearly with symbols? Is there a way to objectively prove that one's own reasoning is perfect? Such questions can be answered with the basics of logical reasoning, including the deconstruction and evaluation of arguments, the structure of logic, and how to assess conclusions using various reasoning methodologies. Topics will include categorical syllogistic logic, propositional logic, and predicate logic which ultimately enable students to assess the strength of arguments in daily life and academic settings. PHILO 103 is cross-listed with CSCI 203. Field trips are not required. (A-F or P/NP) Transfer: (CSU, UC) General Education: Using catalog rights, check catalog for course general education approvals. For further clarification, see a counselor. (C-ID: PHIL 210)

courses.title: Principles and Practices of Teaching Young Children
courses.instructor: Yumi Lee, Brandon Lamar Gilbert, Jeanette K. Fanconi, Katie Quinn, Michael Minnick
courses.description: CLDDV 101-PRINCIPLES AND PRACTICES OF TEACHING YOUNG CHILDREN 3 UNITS Formerly listed as: CLDDV 101: Introduction to Early Childhood Education 54 Lecture Hours, 108 Out-of-Class Hours, Total Student Learning Hours=162 Recommended for Success: Before enrolling in this course, students are strongly advised to satisfactorily complete an MJC English composition course or the equivalent, or to exhibit proficiency in college-level essay writing skills. An examination of the underlying theoretical principles of Developmentally Appropriate Practice in early care and education. Emphasis on the role of the early childhood educator, the importance of teacher-child relationships, and effective teaching strategies and environmental design for supporting development in young children. This course includes a review of the historical roots of early childhood programs and the evolution of the professional practices promoting advocacy, ethics, and professional identity. Field trips might be required. Not repeatable. (A-F or P/NP) Transfer: (CSU) (CC: CHILD 3) (C-ID: ECE 120)

courses.title: Basic Escrow Procedures
courses.description: RLES 392-BASIC ESCROW PROCEDURES, 3 UNITS 54 Lecture Hours, 108 Out-of-Class Hours, Total Student Learning Hours=162 Recommended for Success: Before enrolling in this course, students are strongly advised to satisfactorily complete RLES 380 and/or satisfactorily complete RLES 381. Study of the functions and responsibilities of the escrow holder, including actual preparation of escrow instructions and documents in a typical real estate transaction. Audit, disbursement, the issuance of closing statements and analysis of title insurance policies are covered. This course satisfies the education requirements for the California Real Estate Salesperson and Broker exam and license. Field trips might be required. Not repeatable. (A-F or P/NP)

courses.title: Real Estate Finance
courses.instructor: Ashley Peterson, Need Copy Ins Document (m
courses.description: RLES 384-REAL ESTATE FINANCE 3 UNITS 54 Lecture Hours, 108 Out-of-Class Hours, Total Student Learning Hours=162 Recommended for Success: Before enrolling in this course, students are strongly advised to satisfactorily complete RLES 380 or satisfactorily complete RLES 381. Analysis of real estate financing, including regulations, policies and procedures applicable to financing residential, multi-family, commercial and special purpose properties. Special attention given to the money market, sources of funds, and FHA and VA loans as factors in property financing. This course satisfies the education requirements for the California Real Estate Salesperson and Broker exam and license. Field trips might be required. Not repeatable. (A-F or P/NP)

courses.title: Real Estate Appraisal
courses.description: RLES 385-REAL ESTATE APPRAISAL 3 UNITS Formerly listed as: RLES 385: Real Estate Appraisal, Residential 54 Lecture Hours, 108 Out-of-Class Hours, Total Student Learning Hours=162 Recommended for Success: Before enrolling in this course, students are strongly advised to satisfactorily complete RLES 380 and/or satisfactorily complete RLES 381. Study of the purposes of appraisals, the appraisal process, and the different approaches, methods and techniques used to determine property value based on cost, sales comparison, and income basis. Emphasizes the appraisal of residential single family properties. Covers neighborhood and site analysis, residential style, functional utility, three approaches to value, reconciliation of value indicators, and Uniform Standards of Professional Practice. This course satisfies the education requirements for the California Real Estate Salesperson and Broker exam and license. Field trips might be required. Not repeatable. (A-F or P/NP)

courses.title: Legal Aspects of Real Estate
courses.description: RLES 382-LEGAL ASPECTS OF REAL ESTATE 3 UNITS Formerly listed as: RLES 382: Legal Aspects of Real Estate 1 54 Lecture Hours, 108 Out-of-Class Hours, Total Student Learning Hours=162 Recommended for Success: Before enrolling in this course, students are strongly advised to satisfactorily complete RLES 380. Study of California real estate laws. Topics include: principal legal aspects of ownership, acquisition and transfer of real property, legal descriptions, contracts, escrow procedures, forms of trust and foreclosure, liens, and restrictions, legal instruments, property ownership and management, real estate security devices, property rights, liens and homesteads, landlord-tenant law, land use controls, and title insurance and escrow. This course satisfies the education requirements for the California Real Estate Salesperson and Broker exam and license. Field trips might be required. Not repeatable. (A-F or P/NP)

courses.title: Critical Reading
courses.instructor: Christopher R. Briggs, Sarah B. Hawes
courses.description: READ 184-CRITICAL READING, 3 UNITS 54 Lecture Hours, 108 Out-of-Class Hours, Total Student Learning Hours=162 This course offers students the opportunity to develop, evaluate, and apply critical reading skills and strategies. These strategies include annotating text, separating fact from opinion, analyzing supporting evidence, identifying propaganda techniques and logical fallacies, etc. Students apply attentive, rhetorical, and argumentative reading strategies to a variety of texts. Additional course topics include main ideas, inferred ideas, tone, style, and rhetorical strategies. A minimum of 5,000 words of writing is required. Formal writing includes rhetorical précis and analyses, critical responses, and an argumentative research paper; informal writing includes reading responses, learning reflections, essay drafts, and peer feedback. Field trips are not required. Not repeatable. (A-F Only) Transfer: (CSU, UC) General Education: Using catalog rights, check catalog for course general education approvals. For further clarification, see a counselor.
